planting.note: Plant divisions in spring where some sun will turn the leaves golden.

directions: Leaves emerge chartreuse and ripen to a soft golden-yellow, brightest with a few hours of sun. The slightly puckered surface adds texture to shady borders. Near-white flowers rise above the mound in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in shade with room to bulk up.

directions: Big, rounded, deeply cupped blue leaves with heavy puckering make this a bold shade specimen. Keep it out of hot sun to preserve the blue waxy finish. Near-white flowers rise just above the foliage in early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring with some sun to encourage the fragrant flowers.

directions: A compact hosta with glossy green leaves edged in creamy white, tolerant of more sun than most. It grows quickly and stays neat. Fragrant pale lavender flowers open on upright scapes in late summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a container or the front of a shady bed.

directions: This tiny, award-winning hosta forms a bun of thick, rounded blue-gray leaves shaped like little mouse ears. Its small size suits troughs, containers, and border fronts. Lavender flowers appear on short scapes in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring where afternoon shade and some morning sun meet.

directions: Apple-green leaves with creamy margins form a broad mound that tolerates warmth and some sun. It is a strong grower valued as much for its scent as its foliage. Large, fragrant white flowers open on tall scapes in late summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in bright shade to develop the golden centers.

directions: Centers shift from chartreuse to bright gold as the season warms, framed by blue-green margins. A little morning sun speeds the color change. Pale lavender flowers rise above the mound in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in partial shade for a crisp white edge.

directions: A sport of 'Francee' with darker leaves and a wider, brighter white margin. The heavy leaf substance shrugs off slugs. Lavender flowers rise on upright scapes in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring where the upright, twisted leaves have room.

directions: This large, vase-shaped hosta bears frosty blue-green leaves edged in creamy yellow that twist gracefully. It is slow to establish but becomes a commanding specimen. Pale lavender flowers rise on very tall scapes in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in shade to watch the centers shift color.

directions: Twisted, upswept leaves open with creamy-white centers that darken to green as the season progresses. The swirling habit gives it a lively look in a shady bed. Lavender flowers rise above the mound in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in shade to shield the pale spring centers.

directions: A large sport of 'Blue Angel' with ghostly white-and-green centers ringed by blue margins in spring. The pale tissue greens up as summer heat arrives. Near-white flowers rise on tall scapes in early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in bright shade for the brightest gold.

directions: A small, dense hosta with heart-shaped chartreuse-to-gold leaves, ideal for edging shady paths. It takes some sun, which deepens the color. Pale lavender flowers rise on short scapes in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring at a bed's front or in a container.

directions: This playful mini forms a swirl of narrow, heavily rippled chartreuse leaves. Some morning sun turns the foliage a brighter gold. Lavender flowers rise on wiry scapes in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in bright shade to feed the gold centers.

directions: A small hosta with cupped, shiny gold centers rimmed in dark green that brightens the front of a shady border. A little morning sun intensifies the gold. Lavender flowers rise on short scapes in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in shade with room for a broad blue mound.

directions: Large, rounded, heavily rippled blue leaves form an impressive mound for the shade garden. Keep it out of hot sun so the powdery blue color holds. Near-white flowers rise on tall scapes in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring where the unusual upright leaves can be seen up close.

directions: This one-of-a-kind hosta holds tightly folded, upright leaves edged in a thin gold line, resembling hands pressed together. Its narrow, vertical form adds structure to shady plantings. Lavender flowers rise on tall scapes in late summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in partial shade to hold the frosty blue color.

directions: Frosty blue leaves are edged with a wide margin that opens bright yellow and mellows to cream. This medium hosta keeps a fresh look from spring through fall. Lavender flowers rise above the mound in midsummer.

planting.note: Set out nursery plants or rooted cuttings in spring or early fall.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil with the crown level with the surface and water in well to settle the roots. This classic selection carries unusually large, pure-white flower clusters, so trim it lightly after flowering to keep the habit tidy. Bloom peaks in spring and coats the whole plant for several weeks.

planting.note: Plant container-grown stock in spring for bloom the same season.

directions: Dig a hole slightly wider than the root ball and firm the plant in at its original depth. This is a low, spreading form prized for edging and rock walls; cut it back after flowering to encourage compact new growth. Snow-white flowers smother the foliage through mid-spring.

planting.note: Set out plants in spring so they establish before the first bloom.

directions: Plant in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun and avoid sites where water stands in winter. This selection is grown for its habit of reblooming, giving a second flush of white flowers in autumn on top of the usual spring show. Shear lightly after the spring bloom to tidy the plant and prompt fall flowers.

planting.note: Plant this dwarf form in spring for edging or trough gardens.

directions: Set the compact plant in well-drained soil and space closely for a low white cushion. This dwarf selection stays neat with little pruning, though a light trim after flowering keeps it tight. It flowers heavily in spring and works well tucked into rock crevices.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early autumn in a sunny, sharply drained spot.

directions: Firm the root ball into well-drained soil and keep the crown from sitting too low. This tidy, floriferous form blankets itself in bright white blooms and holds a mound of dark evergreen leaves the rest of the year. Shear back by a third once flowering fades.

planting.note: Set out plants in spring for a dense, low groundcover effect.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, leaving room for the mound to spread. This selection is noted for very heavy, uniform white flowering that nearly hides the foliage at peak. Trim after bloom to keep the cushion compact and encourage fresh growth.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny bed with excellent drainage.

directions: Set the plant at its original soil depth and water gently until established. This vigorous form produces a strong spring flush of white flowers over glossy evergreen foliage. A light shearing after bloom keeps it dense and neat.

planting.note: Plant in spring where its larger flowers can be shown off.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun and avoid winter wet. This selection is grown for its notably large flower heads on a somewhat taller mound. Shear back after the spring display to maintain a rounded shape.

planting.note: Plant this miniature form in troughs or crevices in spring.

directions: Tuck this very low, spreading form into gritty soil in a rock garden or trough. Its tight cushion needs little care beyond a light trim after flowering. Small white flower clusters cover the mat in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with sharp drainage.

directions: Set the plant into free-draining soil and keep it on the drier side once established. Unlike the common white types, this selection opens pale and matures to lavender-purple, giving a two-tone effect. Shear lightly after the main spring flush to keep it tidy.

planting.note: Plant in spring for spring bloom and a possible fall repeat.

directions: Plant in full sun in gritty, well-drained soil and water sparingly once rooted. This form is valued for reblooming, often throwing a second round of white flowers in autumn. Trim after the spring flush to encourage the later show.

planting.note: Sow or plant in spring in a lean, gritty rock-garden site.

directions: Surface-sow seed onto sharply drained, gravelly soil and keep barely moist until germination. This dwarf alpine species forms a very low evergreen cushion suited to troughs, walls, and rock gardens. White flowers appear in late spring, and the plant resents rich soil or standing water.

planting.note: Sow after frost danger passes; this species is less cold-hardy than most.

directions: Sow onto the surface of well-drained soil in a warm, sunny spot and keep lightly moist. This tender species is grown for flowers that open pale pink to lilac and fade paler as they age. Provide winter protection at the cold edge of its range, and trim after bloom to keep it compact.

planting.note: Start indoors several weeks before the last frost, then move out after it passes.

directions: Sow onto the surface of a fine seed mix under warmth and transplant seedlings once frost has passed. This selection is grown for large pink-flushed flower heads and can bloom well its first year from an early sowing. Give it full sun and lean, well-drained soil, and deadhead to extend the display.

planting.note: Sow or plant in spring into a sunny, gritty rock-garden bed.

directions: Sow seed thinly on the surface of sharply drained soil and keep it just moist until sprouting. This low, mat-forming alpine species carries white flowers, sometimes with a faint lilac tint, in spring. It performs best in lean soil with full sun and dislikes winter wet.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring and again in succession for a longer show.

directions: Scatter seed over prepared soil and cover very lightly, thinning seedlings once they are a couple inches tall. This quick, fragrant annual bears domed clusters in white, pink, lavender, and rose. Sow every few weeks in cool weather, since flowering slows in summer heat; expect blooms about two months from sowing.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring for a low, colorful edging.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and keep the soil moist until the seedlings emerge, then thin to stand a few inches apart. This dwarf strain blooms in a blend of white, pink, rose, and lilac and is well suited to borders and containers. Deadhead or shear lightly to prompt a fresh flush during cool weather.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring where the plants are to flower.

directions: Sow seed shallowly into well-drained soil and thin seedlings once established. This annual differs from globe candytuft in its upright, hyacinth-like spikes of fragrant white flowers that lengthen as they open. It makes a good cut flower; sow in cool weather and cut stems as the spikes begin to color.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring for tall white spikes and cutting stems.

directions: Sow seed lightly in well-drained soil and thin the seedlings to give each plant room to branch. This selection is grown for its clean white, upright, fragrant flower spikes that are excellent for cutting. Keep it well watered in cool weather and harvest stems as the lower florets open.

planting.note: Start seed in spring or plant divisions where the bold clump has room to develop.

directions: Sow shallowly in cool conditions and grow on in deep, fertile, moisture-retentive ground. Site this architectural giant at the back of a border where its coarse basal leaves and towering stems will not crowd smaller plants. Broad heads of narrow-rayed yellow flowers appear through summer and are much visited by bees.

planting.note: Plant divisions or pot-grown stock in spring into deep, retentive soil.

directions: Set the crown at soil level in rich ground that stays reliably moist through summer. This selection is grown from divisions to keep its uniform, generous flowering, so lift and split congested clumps in spring every few years. Radiant golden-yellow blooms with fine rays crown the tall stems in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in early spring and set young plants out after frost.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep it cool and evenly moist until sprouts appear. Grow in deep, fertile soil that does not dry out, in full sun or a little afternoon shade. Large, deep golden-orange flowers with slender rays open on stout stems in mid to late summer, making a strong single show.

planting.note: Start indoors in early spring or sow outdoors once soil warms; set plants at the front of a border.

directions: Barely cover the fine seed and keep it lightly moist until germination. This tidy, drought-tolerant dwarf thrives in average, well-drained soil and full sun, needing no staking. A long summer display of narrow-rayed golden daisies covers the neat, grassy-leaved mounds; shear spent flowers to keep it fresh.

planting.note: Plant divisions or pot-grown stock in spring at the edge of a bed or in a rock garden.

directions: Set divisions in well-drained, average soil and full sun where their small size can be appreciated. This especially compact form makes a low, dense cushion ideal for edging or a sunny rock garden. Bright yellow daisy flowers smother the mound through much of the summer.

planting.note: Plant spring divisions or container stock into well-drained soil at the front of a border.

directions: Plant in average, freely draining soil and full sun, spacing to let the compact clumps knit together. This selection is prized for its abundant, evenly gold, star-shaped flowers held on slender stems. Blooms carry through the summer above narrow deep-green foliage; trim after flowering to tidy the plant.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in spring or set out divisions into a sunny, retentive bed.

directions: Surface-sow and keep cool and moist until germination, then grow on in fertile, moisture-retentive soil. Give this clump-forming perennial full sun and room for its upright stems. Fringed, deep orange-yellow flowers with very fine rays open from felted buds in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Sow in spring into deep soil; give the tall clump plenty of room.

directions: Sow shallowly in cool spring conditions and grow on in deep, rich, moisture-retentive soil. Closely resembling elecampane, this tall species forms a big rosette of coarse leaves before sending up leafy flowering stems. Yellow rayed flowers open in summer, and the aromatic taproot is harvested from established plants in autumn.

planting.note: Sow in spring into a moist, sunny spot; tolerates damp ground.

directions: Sow shallowly and keep the soil moist, as this species favors damp meadows and streamsides. Grow in sun or light shade in ground that stays reliably wet, where it will spread gently. Small golden daisies with slender rays open over a long summer season above narrow leaves.

planting.note: Sow in spring into average or moist soil in full sun.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep it moist until it sprouts. This adaptable meadow species grows in average to damp soils and tolerates lime, forming upright stems clad in willow-like leaves. Bright yellow daisies with narrow rays appear through mid and late summer.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in spring and plant out into sharply drained soil or a rock garden.

directions: Surface-sow and keep cool and lightly moist, as this alpine germinates best in cool conditions. Grow in gritty, very well-drained soil in full sun, ideal for a trough, scree, or rock garden. Nearly stalkless golden daisies sit right on the low, tufted cushion of leaves in early summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in spring or sow outdoors after soil warms; plant into well-drained soil.

directions: Barely cover the fine seed and keep it lightly moist until germination. This drought-tolerant species suits average, well-drained soil in full sun and forms neat, softly hairy clumps. Golden-yellow daisies open above the grey-green foliage in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in summer to allow a strong root system before frost.

directions: Press the rhizome barely into gritty, well-drained soil with the top exposed to sun. Keep it on the dry side and feed lightly in early spring and again after the first flush. This reliable rebloomer opens pure white ruffled flowers in late spring and often again in late summer or fall.

planting.note: Late-summer planting gives roots time to anchor before cold.

directions: Settle the rhizome just at the surface in a sunny, freely draining bed and firm the soil around the roots. Avoid mulch directly over the rhizome and keep water sparing through summer. Look for large, heavily ruffled coral-pink blooms in mid spring on this Dykes Medal favorite.

planting.note: Set rhizomes in summer while the soil is warm.

directions: Plant with the rhizome top exposed and the roots fanned into loose, sunny soil. Water to establish, then let the ground dry between soakings. The near-black, deep ebony-purple flowers appear in mid to late spring and glow best against lighter companions.

planting.note: Plant in late summer for bloom the following spring.

directions: Bury only the roots and leave the rhizome shoulder in the light, spacing plants to keep air moving. Give full sun and restrained watering to prevent rot. Tall stems carry richly ruffled, dark blue-purple flowers in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Summer planting lets roots settle before winter.

directions: Firm the rhizome into gritty soil with its top just breaking the surface. Keep the crown dry and feed a low-nitrogen fertilizer in early spring. This Dykes Medal winner opens striking bicolor flowers with yellow standards over blue-violet falls in mid spring.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in late summer in a sunny spot.

directions: Set the rhizome shallowly with roots spread in well-drained ground and the top facing the sun. Water sparingly and remove old foliage in fall to reduce disease. The crisp white flowers are boldly edged and stitched in deep violet, a classic plicata blooming in mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in summer so clumps establish before frost.

directions: Nestle the rhizome at the surface in sharply drained, sunny soil and keep it lean. Avoid overhead watering and divide when clumps crowd. Large, velvety deep blue-purple flowers with heavy substance appear in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Late-summer planting is ideal for root establishment.

directions: Plant the rhizome just below the surface with its top exposed and roots fanned downward. Provide full sun and a dry summer rest after bloom. Ruffled medium-blue flowers with a small white flash at the beard open in mid to late spring on tall, well-branched stems.

planting.note: Set rhizomes in summer for spring flowering.

directions: Press the rhizome into loose, sunny soil with the top just showing and firm the roots. Keep watering light and clear away debris around the crown. Ruffled white flowers washed and stitched with violet-purple appear in mid spring on this Dykes Medal plicata.

planting.note: Plant in late summer while soil is warm.

directions: Settle the rhizome at the surface in fast-draining ground and leave the top uncovered. Water to establish, then keep it dry through summer. Large ruffled flowers in pale silvery-blue open in mid to late spring on tall stems.

planting.note: Summer planting suits this vigorous grower.

directions: Plant shallowly with the rhizome crown in the light and roots spread in dry, sunny soil. Feed sparingly and divide crowded clumps after flowering. Deep, near-black purple flowers with a fragrant scent open in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in late summer for next-spring bloom.

directions: Set the rhizome barely into gritty soil with its top exposed and roots anchored below. Keep the site sunny and on the dry side. This border iris shows purple flowers randomly streaked and splashed with white in mid spring, a distinctive broken-color pattern.

planting.note: Plant this heirloom in late summer in full sun.

directions: Firm the rhizome into well-drained soil with its top just at the surface. Water lightly to start and let it dry between rains. This vintage Dykes Medal amoena carries white standards above deep purple falls edged in white, opening in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant in summer to root in before cold weather.

directions: Place the rhizome at the surface in sunny, sharply drained soil with roots spread wide. Keep watering restrained and remove spent stalks after bloom. Ruffled white standards over rich red-violet falls open in mid spring on this Dykes Medal selection.

planting.note: Late-summer planting readies clumps for spring.

directions: Set the rhizome shallowly with its top exposed and firm the fanned roots in dry, sunny ground. Avoid mulching over the crown and water only when needed. Soft ruffled lavender-blue flowers appear in mid to late spring on this Dykes Medal winner.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in summer for the strongest roots.

directions: Bury only the roots and let the rhizome shoulder catch the sun in loose, draining soil. Water to establish and keep summers dry. Wavy, deeply ruffled medium-blue flowers open in mid to late spring on this award-winning iris.

planting.note: Plant this heirloom in late summer in a sunny bed.

directions: Press the rhizome into gritty soil with the top just visible and roots spread below. Keep it sunny and dry and divide every few years. Deep golden-yellow flowers glow in mid spring on this classic Dykes Medal iris.

planting.note: Plant in summer so tall clumps settle before frost.

directions: Set the rhizome at the surface in freely draining soil and firm the roots in full sun. Water sparingly and stake tall stems in windy sites. Bold flowers with golden-yellow standards over mahogany-red falls open in mid spring.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in summer for spring bloom.

directions: Settle the rhizome shallowly with the crown exposed and roots anchored in dry, sunny ground. Keep water minimal and clean up old leaves each fall. Unusual pale greenish-tan flowers with long horned beards open in mid to late spring on this Dykes Medal iris.

planting.note: Late-summer planting suits this ruffled variety.

directions: Plant with the rhizome top in the light and roots fanned into sharply drained soil. Provide full sun and let the ground dry between waterings. Ruffled white-and-violet flowers finished with distinctive horned beards open in mid spring on this Dykes Medal iris.

planting.note: Plant in summer to establish before winter.

directions: Set the rhizome barely below the surface with its top showing and roots spread in draining soil. Keep it sunny and water lightly. Deeply ruffled medium-blue flowers with fine substance open in mid to late spring on this Dykes Medal winner.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in late summer in full sun.

directions: Firm the rhizome into gritty, sunny soil with the crown exposed and roots below. Water to establish and keep summers dry. Tall, well-branched stems carry large ruffled deep-blue flowers in mid to late spring on this Dykes Medal iris.

planting.note: Plant in summer so this rebloomer roots well.

directions: Set the rhizome at the surface in sunny, well-drained soil and firm the roots. Feed after the spring flush to encourage a second round. Clear yellow flowers open in mid spring and often return again in fall in mild regions.

planting.note: Plant in summer to support strong rebloom.

directions: Plant the rhizome shallowly with the top exposed in draining, sunny soil. Water and feed lightly after bloom to fuel a second flush. Deep violet-purple flowers open in mid spring and reliably rebloom in early fall in many climates.

planting.note: Plant in summer for spring and possible fall bloom.

directions: Set the rhizome at the surface in sunny, sharply drained soil with roots fanned out. Keep water light and feed after the spring show. Soft flowers with cream standards over apricot-pink falls open in mid spring and often rebloom in fall.

planting.note: Plant in summer for reliable spring and fall bloom.

directions: Press the rhizome into loose soil with the top just showing and roots below the surface. Give full sun and water after bloom to prompt rebloom. Pale flowers with near-white standards over soft blue-lavender falls open in mid spring and frequently again in fall.

planting.note: Plant in summer to feed a strong rebloom cycle.

directions: Settle the rhizome at the surface in draining, sunny ground and firm the roots. Water and feed after the first flowers to drive reblooming. Deep violet-purple flowers open in mid spring and return in late summer to fall in many gardens.

planting.note: Plant in summer for spring and autumn flowers.

directions: Plant the rhizome shallowly with the crown exposed in gritty, sunny soil. Keep it lean and water after bloom to encourage the fall flush. White flowers finely stitched in violet, a plicata pattern, open in mid spring and often rebloom in fall.

planting.note: Plant in late summer in full sun.

directions: Set the rhizome at the surface in sharply drained soil with roots spread and firmed. Water sparingly and divide when clumps crowd. Velvety deep red-brown to oxblood flowers open in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in summer for spring bloom.

directions: Firm the rhizome into draining, sunny soil with the top exposed and roots below. Keep summers dry after flowering. Rich, glowing red-brown flowers with bronze beards open in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant in late summer in a sunny, open spot.

directions: Set the rhizome at the surface in gritty soil and fan the roots into full sun. Water lightly and clean up foliage in fall. Large ruffled flowers in pale silvery ice-blue open in mid to late spring on tall stems.

planting.note: Plant in summer for spring flowering.

directions: Plant the rhizome shallowly with the crown showing in draining, sunny soil. Keep water restrained and divide every few years. Clear medium-blue flowers with matching beards open in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant in late summer in full sun.

directions: Set the rhizome at the surface in sharply drained soil with roots firmed below. Water lightly and keep the crown dry. Ruffled flowers in soft raspberry-pink with a lighter edge open in mid spring on this compact grower.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in summer in a sunny bed.

directions: Firm the rhizome into draining soil with the top exposed and roots spread. Give full sun and water only when dry. Ruffled medium blue-violet flowers with white-based beards open in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant in late summer for spring bloom.

directions: Set the rhizome at the surface in gritty, sunny soil and firm the roots. Water sparingly through summer. Soft, self-colored pale pink flowers with tangerine beards open in mid spring on this widely grown iris.

planting.note: Plant in summer in full sun for spring flowers.

directions: Plant the rhizome shallowly with the crown in the light and roots below. Keep it lean and dry between waterings. Earthy flowers with tan-gold standards over rosy-brown falls and a plicata edge open in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in late summer in full sun.

directions: Set the rhizome at the surface in sharply drained soil with roots firmed below. Keep the crown dry and remove old stalks after bloom. White flowers boldly speckled and washed with violet, a striking plicata, open in mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in summer to establish before winter.

directions: Firm the rhizome into draining, sunny soil with the top just showing. Water to establish and keep summers dry. Deep, richly saturated navy-blue flowers with fine ruffling open in mid to late spring on tall stems.

planting.note: Plant this heirloom in late summer in full sun.

directions: Set the rhizome shallowly with its top exposed in gritty, draining soil. Keep it sunny and water only when dry. This vintage Dykes Medal iris carries blue-violet standards over deeper purple falls in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant this antique iris in late summer.

directions: Firm the rhizome into draining soil with the crown exposed to the sun. Keep water sparing and divide clumps every few years. This heirloom offers white flowers with faint violet veining on the falls in mid spring.

planting.note: Plant this old variety in late summer in full sun.

directions: Set the rhizome at the surface in lean, draining soil and fan the roots in full sun. Water lightly and keep the crown dry. This long-cultivated iris bears soft, pale lemon-yellow flowers with a gentle fragrance in mid spring.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed in autumn or lift and divide established clumps in early spring.

directions: This close relative of Siberian iris wants moist, fertile ground and full sun to light shade. Plant divisions shallowly and mulch to conserve moisture through summer. Rich red-violet flowers with rounded falls appear in late spring on unbranched stems.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in rich, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Set the crown an inch or two deep and water well until the clump takes hold. This vigorous old favorite forms tall, arching grassy foliage and needs dividing only every several years. Deep violet-blue flowers crowd the stems in late spring above the leaves.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early autumn in fertile, moist soil.

directions: Plant shallowly with the fan of leaves upright and keep the ground evenly moist. This bicolor sets white standards over soft yellow falls, one of the first widely grown yellow Siberians. Blooms arrive in late spring and often rebloom lightly if kept watered.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in moist, humus-rich soil.

directions: Tuck divisions in just below the soil line and water regularly the first season. This old, hardy selection carries pure white flowers with a faint yellow throat. Bloom comes in late spring above tidy grassy clumps.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early autumn in rich, moist ground.

directions: Plant shallowly and mulch to hold moisture around the roots. The ruffled reddish-purple falls are marked with gold and blue veining near the throat. Expect flowers in late spring on a compact, well-behaved clump.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in fertile, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Set the crown just under the surface and keep new plants watered until established. This landmark selection has rounded, flaring white falls that opened the door to modern Siberian breeding. Flowers appear in late spring on strong, upright stems.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early autumn in moist, rich soil.

directions: Plant the fan upright with the crown lightly covered and water in well. This tall, old-fashioned selection bears soft sky-blue flowers with a small white and gold signal. Blooms rise well above the foliage in late spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in fertile, moist ground.

directions: Plant shallowly and keep evenly moist through the first summer. Rosy lavender-pink flowers with a lighter signal glow against the grassy foliage. Bloom season is late spring into early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early autumn in rich, moisture-holding soil.

directions: Set divisions just below the surface and water regularly while they root. Medium blue flowers are finely rimmed with a thin silvery-white edge that gives the plant its name. Look for bloom in late spring above neat clumps.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in moist, fertile soil.

directions: Plant shallowly and mulch to keep the roots cool and moist. The small blue flowers have white falls netted with fine blue veins, resembling a swarm of butterflies. Abundant bloom comes in late spring on airy stems.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early autumn in rich, moist ground.

directions: Plant the crown lightly covered and keep watered until established. Deep wine-red to purple falls carry a striking white signal at the throat. Flowers open in late spring above grassy foliage.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in fertile, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Plant shallowly and water in thoroughly. White standards top creamy falls that flush pale yellow toward the center. Blooms appear in late spring on upright stems.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early autumn in moist, rich soil.

directions: Set divisions just under the surface and keep the soil moist through summer. Soft pale blue flowers have gracefully flaring, lightly ruffled falls. Bloom comes in late spring above the foliage.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in fertile, moist ground.

directions: Plant shallowly and water regularly until the clump is settled. Rounded, ruffled flowers blend pink, lavender, and cream tones for a soft effect. Flowers appear in late spring to early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early autumn in rich, moist soil.

directions: Plant the crown lightly covered and keep evenly moist the first year. Deep purple falls contrast with bright yellow style arms for a two-tone look. Bloom season is late spring on strong stems.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in moist, fertile soil.

directions: Plant shallowly and mulch lightly to retain moisture. Delicate pink-lavender flowers give a hazy, soft-colored effect in the border. Flowers open in late spring above grassy clumps.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early autumn in rich, moisture-holding soil.

directions: Set divisions just below the surface and water in well. Clean white flowers with flaring falls and a yellow signal read crisply in the garden. Bloom comes in late spring on tall stems.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in fertile, moist ground.

directions: Plant the fan upright with the crown lightly covered and water thoroughly. Large ruffled flowers of soft pink-lavender make a graceful display. Blooms rise above the foliage in late spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early autumn in moist, rich soil.

directions: Plant shallowly and keep watered until established. Rich medium-blue flowers with a modest signal make a reliable, colorful clump. Flowers appear in late spring above the leaves.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in fertile, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Set divisions just under the surface and water in well. Ruffled violet-blue flowers are held on compact, well-branched stems. Look for bloom in late spring on a tidy clump.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in acidic, wet, humus-rich soil.

directions: Plant in constantly moist, lime-free soil and water generously through the season. Grown as much for its cream-and-green striped foliage as for its rosy-purple flowers, it lights up a pondside planting. Blooms open in early to mid summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early autumn in acidic, moist soil.

directions: Keep the roots wet during growth and bloom, and avoid any lime. Soft rose-pink flowers with a single set of falls have an elegant, understated form. Flowers open in early to mid summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in acidic, wet, fertile soil.

directions: Grow in rich, boggy soil and keep it wet through summer. Large white flowers are peppered and edged with rosy-purple flecks and veining. Broad blooms appear in early to mid summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early autumn in acidic, moisture-rich soil.

directions: Plant in wet, lime-free ground and never let it dry out during bloom. Large ruffled flowers in rich rose-purple washed with white make a bold statement. Flowers open in early to mid summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in acidic, wet soil.

directions: Keep the soil constantly moist and acidic for best performance. White flowers are heavily veined and streaked with violet-purple and carry a yellow signal. Broad, flat blooms open in early to mid summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early autumn in acidic, moist, rich soil.

directions: Grow in wet, lime-free soil and water heavily during bud and bloom. Deep violet-purple falls are rimmed with a clean white picotee edge. Large flowers appear in early to mid summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in acidic, wet soil.

directions: Keep the roots wet and the soil free of lime for strong growth. Ruffled white flowers are edged and lightly veined in rosy-red. Flowers open in early to mid summer above upright foliage.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early autumn in acidic, moist ground.

directions: Plant in rich, boggy soil and keep it wet through summer. Bold red-purple flowers show bright golden signals and pale veining. Large blooms open in early to mid summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in acidic, wet, humus-rich soil.

directions: Keep the soil constantly moist and lime-free for best bloom. Large flowers of deep violet-blue have a velvety texture and a small yellow signal. Broad blooms appear in early to mid summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early autumn in acidic, moist soil.

directions: Grow in wet, fertile soil and avoid lime near the roots. Deep purple flowers with prominent white veining have a graceful, cascading form. Flowers open in early to mid summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in acidic, wet, rich soil.

directions: Keep the soil moist and lime-free, watering heavily during bloom. Very large soft pink flowers with a frosted, silvery cast are held on tall stems. Broad blooms open in early to mid summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early autumn in acidic, moist soil.

directions: Plant in wet, humus-rich soil and keep it evenly moist. Rich blue-violet flowers have a deep, saturated color and a small golden signal. Flowers open in early to mid summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in acidic, wet, fertile soil.

directions: Keep the roots wet during the growing season and never add lime. This old, treasured selection bears pure white double flowers with bright gold signals. Large blooms appear in early to mid summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early autumn in acidic, moist ground.

directions: Grow in constantly moist, lime-free soil for best results. Purple flowers are strikingly veined and edged in white with a yellow signal. Large blooms open in early to mid summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in acidic, wet soil.

directions: Keep the soil wet and free of lime through the season. Purple-and-white patterned flowers give a playful, marbled effect. Broad blooms open in early to mid summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early autumn in acidic, moist, rich soil.

directions: Keep the roots wet during growth and bloom and avoid lime. Soft rosy-pink flowers have a gentle, luminous quality in the garden. Flowers open in early to mid summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in acidic, wet soil.

directions: Grow in rich, boggy soil kept wet through summer and free of lime. Ruffled lavender-pink flowers have a full, rounded form. Large blooms open in early to mid summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early autumn in moist, fertile soil.

directions: Set divisions just below the surface and keep watered while establishing. Creamy white flowers with a yellow signal make a soft, pale-toned clump. Bloom comes in late spring above grassy foliage.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in rich, moist soil.

directions: Plant shallowly and mulch to conserve moisture. Deep ruby-purple flowers glow with a jewel-like tone in the border. Flowers open in late spring to early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early autumn in moist, fertile ground.

directions: Set the crown lightly covered and water in well. Clean white flowers with a hint of gold at the throat make a bright, airy clump. Blooms rise above the foliage in late spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in rich, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Plant shallowly and keep evenly moist through the first summer. Soft lavender-pink flowers are produced in generous numbers on branched stems. Bloom season is late spring into early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, acidic, leafy shade; shelter from wind and hot sun.

directions: Give this Korean species the same cool, moist, woodland conditions as its Japanese cousin, keeping the crown at soil level. Its flowers are held slightly more upright and open a touch earlier. Watch for the buttery-yellow bells opening on tall arching stems in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in boggy or streamside soil in light shade.

directions: Position in deep, moisture-retentive soil at the edge of a pond or in a low, damp bed. Keep roots cool and never let the ground bake dry, or the toothed leaves will scorch. Slender yellow flower spikes on near-black stems appear in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, moist soil in light to partial shade.

directions: Set in deep, humus-rich soil that stays damp all season, ideally near water. Young spring shoots of this species are eaten as a vegetable in parts of East Asia, but grown ornamentally it is left to flower. Yellow blooms rise in loose spikes during summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, rich soil sheltered from wind.

directions: Grow in cool, constantly moist ground where the large rounded leaves form a bold clump. Provide shelter so the tall stems are not blown over. Dense conical spikes of yellow daisies open on stout stems in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in deep, wet, fertile soil in partial shade.

directions: Give this vigorous species a large, permanently damp site where its round, long-stalked leaves have room to spread. Keep well watered through dry spells to avoid wilting. Tall branching spikes of yellow flowers appear over a long midsummer season.

planting.note: Plant in spring in boggy ground or beside water in sun to light shade.

directions: This wetland species thrives in genuinely wet, peaty soil and tolerates more sun where the roots stay saturated. Sow fresh seed in autumn or divide in spring. Narrow racemes of yellow flowers open from midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, rich soil in partial shade; good for smaller gardens.

directions: A more compact species, well suited to smaller damp borders in fertile, moisture-retentive soil. Keep evenly watered and shaded from harsh sun. Loose clusters of orange-yellow daisies top the neat mounds of kidney-shaped leaves in summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in deep, moist, fertile soil in partial shade.

directions: This robust garden hybrid combines large heart-shaped leaves with tall flowering stems and needs constantly moist, rich soil. Propagate by spring division since the hybrid does not come true from seed. Broad heads of orange-yellow daisies rise well clear of the foliage in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in rich, permanently moist soil with midday shade.

directions: Grow for the striking foliage, which emerges deep mahogany-purple and matures to green above rich beetroot-red undersides. Keep the soil constantly damp so the big leaves stay turgid through summer heat. Clusters of bright orange daisies open above the mound in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in fertile, wet soil in partial shade.

directions: Very similar to 'Desdemona', with large purple-flushed leaves and dark red undersides on a bold clump. Site in deep, moisture-holding soil out of scorching sun to keep the foliage fresh. Vivid orange flower heads rise above the leaves in late summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in rich, reliably moist soil with afternoon shade.

directions: Prized for the darkest foliage of the group, with glossy chocolate-maroon leaves that hold their color well. Provide constant moisture and shelter from midday sun so the leaves never wilt or fade. Orange daisy flowers on dark stems make a strong contrast in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in wet, fertile soil in light shade.

directions: A dark-leaved selection with rounded near-black foliage that anchors a damp shade border. Keep the ground consistently moist to prevent midday flagging. Bright orange daisies open above the dusky leaves in late summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in deep, moist, humus-rich soil in partial shade.

directions: Grown for dark purple-black leaves that make a bold foil in a moist woodland setting. Never let the soil dry out, and shade from strong afternoon sun. Orange-gold daisy flowers rise on dark stems during mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in fertile, permanently moist soil in partial shade.

directions: An award-winning selection grown for its dramatic vertical flower spikes above jagged, toothed leaves. Give it a wet, rich site where the roots never dry, ideally near water. Tall black-stemmed spires studded with small yellow flowers shoot up in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, fertile soil in partial shade.

directions: A more compact take on 'The Rocket', better scaled for smaller damp borders and containers. Keep the soil evenly wet and out of harsh sun to protect the deeply cut leaves. Slim yellow flower spikes on dark stems appear from mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in rich, consistently moist soil in partial shade.

directions: A sturdy compact hybrid with thick flower spikes held on dark stems above serrated leaves. Its shorter, stouter habit resists flopping better than the taller rockets. Provide steady moisture and enjoy the yellow spires from midsummer onward.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in deep, wet, fertile soil sheltered from wind.

directions: A stately award-winning hybrid with large rounded leaves and tall, statuesque flower spikes. Give it a big, permanently damp site and shelter so the stems stay upright. Conical spires of orange-gold daisies rise high above the foliage in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, rich soil in partial shade; suits smaller gardens.

directions: A compact selection with toothed leaves that flush deep purple-brown when young. Its neat size fits well in smaller damp borders kept steadily moist. Yellow-orange daisy flowers open above the dusky foliage in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, consistently moist soil in partial shade.

directions: A dwarf, compact hybrid with attractively serrated leaves that stay tidy in a small moist bed. Keep the soil damp and shaded from the hottest sun. Orange-yellow daisies appear on short stems through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in deep, wet, fertile soil with room to spread.

directions: Grown for its enormous, boldly toothed leaves that make a tropical statement in a damp border. Give it a large, permanently moist site and shelter from wind that could tatter the foliage. Big orange daisy heads rise above the leaves in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in rich, moist soil in partial shade; ideal for containers.

directions: A true dwarf with glossy, dark purple-tinged rounded leaves that suits pots and the front of a damp bed. Keep the compost or soil evenly moist and never bone dry. Surprisingly large orange daisies open just above the low mound in summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in deep, moist, fertile soil in partial shade.

directions: A robust hybrid combining bold rounded leaves with tall, showy flower spikes. Site in a large, permanently damp bed and keep well watered in dry weather. Dense spires of golden-yellow daisies rise above the foliage in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Set plants out after the last spring frost into freely draining ground.

directions: Barely cover seed and keep at room temperature until sprouts appear in two to four weeks. Give the crown a full-sun spot with sharp drainage, and cut faded pokers to the base to tidy the clump. Bicolor spikes of coral-red fading to yellow rise in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring once frost has passed; it tolerates cold better than most.

directions: Start seed indoors under a thin soil cover and grow on before setting out the blue-grey rosettes. This species handles damp winters better than others but still wants a sunny, open site. Salmon-and-cream flower spikes appear in late summer and early autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring; the slender grassy foliage suits smaller borders.

directions: Cover seed lightly and grow seedlings on before planting the fine-leaved clumps in a sunny bed. This graceful species needs little fuss beyond good drainage and a spring tidy. Fiery orange-red pokers glow above the grassy foliage from late summer.

planting.note: Give this bold-leaved species room; plant out after frost.

directions: Start seed indoors and pot on the strong seedlings, which form a dramatic rosette of broad, aloe-like leaves. Grown as much for its architectural foliage as its bloom, it wants sun and sharp drainage. Coral-and-yellow spikes rise from the center in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring into well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Sow seed under a light dusting of grit and grow on before planting out the slender clumps. This species offers soft citrus-yellow pokers on airy stems and asks only for sun and drainage. Pale lemon spikes appear through summer.

planting.note: Plant after frost; protect the crown in colder gardens.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep warm until germination, then grow on before planting. This distinctive species spaces its curved coral tubular flowers loosely along the stem rather than in a dense poker. Widely spaced orange-red flowers open over a long summer season.

planting.note: Plant divisions or pots in spring after the last frost.

directions: Set the crown at soil level in a sunny, well-drained spot and water until established. This compact selection stays neat and reblooms if spent spikes are removed. Short, densely packed orange-to-yellow pokers appear from early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring once frost danger has passed.

directions: Plant the crown level with the soil surface and give it a full day of sun. Deadhead spent pokers to keep the clump blooming and tidy the foliage in spring. Warm orange-red spikes stand tall from early to midsummer.

planting.note: Set out in spring into free-draining ground.

directions: Plant the crown at ground level in full sun with good drainage underneath. Remove faded spikes and clear old leaves in early spring for the tidiest clump. Buds open from green through to clear lemon-yellow across summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring after the last frost in a sunny border.

directions: Settle the crown level with the soil and firm gently, then water through the first season. Cut old flower stems out at the base and comb away dead foliage each spring. Glowing yellow-orange pokers on dark stems open through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring into a warm, well-drained spot.

directions: Plant the crown at soil level and mulch lightly, keeping the center free of soggy mulch. Deadhead through the season to prolong the display. Soft greenish-yellow pokers age to cream from late summer into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a classic bicolor for the sunny border.

directions: Set the crown level with the surrounding soil in full sun with sharp drainage. Snip out spent pokers and remove tatty leaves before new spring growth. This traditional variety shows scarlet buds opening to bright yellow in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a tall, late, cool-toned selection.

directions: Plant the substantial crown at soil level and stake in exposed sites. Cut back the tall old stems each spring and keep the base clear. Buds tinged pale primrose open to icy cream on stately spikes in early autumn.

planting.note: Give this large form plenty of room; plant after frost.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and grow seedlings on for a season before planting the vigorous clumps. This towering form makes a bold late-summer statement and needs staking in windy gardens. Massive orange-red pokers rise well above the foliage in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a compact, long-blooming dwarf.

directions: Plant the crown level with the soil in a sunny, well-drained bed and water in well. This small, reblooming variety flowers over a long stretch if deadheaded. Rich papaya-orange pokers appear from early summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring for a compact, repeat-flowering clump.

directions: Set the crown at soil level in full sun and keep drainage sharp beneath it. Remove spent spikes to encourage a fresh flush through the season. Warm tangerine-orange pokers rise on tidy stems from early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a dwarf yellow for the front of the border.

directions: Plant the crown flush with the soil in full sun and water through establishment. This compact selection reblooms well when old spikes are trimmed away. Clean pineapple-yellow pokers appear over a long summer season.

planting.note: Plant in spring for a compact, fiery front-border clump.

directions: Plant the crown at soil level in a sunny spot with excellent drainage. Deadhead regularly to keep the reblooming display coming. Vivid red-orange pokers glow above the grassy foliage from early summer onward.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a neat lemon-yellow dwarf.

directions: Set the crown level with the soil in full sun and keep it well drained. Snip off finished pokers to prompt further bloom through summer. Slender lemon-yellow spikes rise above tidy foliage over a long season.

planting.note: Plant in spring for a compact bicolor clump.

directions: Plant the crown flush with the soil in full sun with sharp drainage. Remove spent spikes to keep the dwarf clump reblooming. Soft creamy-orange bicolor pokers open through the summer months.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a compact seed-raised variety.

directions: Barely cover seed and keep warm, then grow seedlings on to plant out compact clumps. This early, free-flowering selection often blooms in its first full season. Bright red-and-yellow bicolor pokers appear from early summer.

planting.note: Plant seed-grown clumps out in spring after frost.

directions: Sow indoors with a light covering and grow on before planting into a sunny bed. This mixed seed strain gives a lively spread of warm colors and often flowers the first year. Pokers in shades of red, orange, and yellow rise through summer.

planting.note: Give this tall, unusual variety room; plant in spring.

directions: Plant the crown at soil level in full sun and stake tall stems in exposed gardens. Cut old flower stalks to the base and clear spent leaves in spring. Cool jade-green buds open to creamy white on statuesque spikes in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a dainty dwarf for smaller spaces.

directions: Set the crown level with the soil in a sunny spot with grassy room to arch. Deadhead spent spikes and comb out old foliage in early spring. Soft buff buds open to pale ivory-yellow on slender stems in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring for a distinctive two-tone spike.

directions: Plant the crown at soil level in full sun with reliable drainage. Remove spent stems and tidy leaves in spring for the best clump. Buff-brown buds open to creamy white, giving a toffee-tipped effect in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; prized for dark stems and long bloom.

directions: Plant the crown flush with the soil in full sun and firm gently. Deadhead through the long season and clear old foliage each spring. Bronze-tinted stems carry apricot buds opening to cream over many weeks in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring into a warm, sunny border.

directions: Set the crown level with the soil in full sun and water until settled. Remove finished pokers to prolong the show and tidy leaves in spring. Glowing golden-orange spikes with a warm apricot cast rise in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a long-flowering mid-size variety.

directions: Plant the crown at soil level in full sun with good drainage. Deadhead regularly to stretch the flowering season into autumn. Coral-pink buds open to soft salmon-and-cream over a long summer display.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a compact, long-blooming red.

directions: Plant the crown level with the soil in full sun and keep drainage sharp. Snip off spent pokers to encourage a long run of bloom. Compact coral-red spikes appear from early summer well into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a tall, clear-yellow selection.

directions: Plant the crown at soil level in full sun and stake in windy sites. Remove old flower stems and clear the base in spring. Bright buttercup-yellow pokers stand tall in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring into free-draining, sunny ground.

directions: Set the crown flush with the soil in full sun and water through establishment. Deadhead to keep the clump neat and blooming. Warm golden-yellow pokers open over a long midsummer season.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a compact late-flowering bicolor.

directions: Plant the crown at soil level in full sun with sharp drainage. Remove spent spikes and tidy foliage in early spring. Orange buds tipped with yellow give a two-tone glow in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring for a warm-toned mid-border clump.

directions: Plant the crown level with the soil in full sun and keep it well drained. Deadhead spent pokers to extend the flowering run. Amber-orange buds open to soft cream, giving a smoky bicolor effect in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a bold, hot-colored variety.

directions: Plant the crown at soil level in full sun with sharp drainage beneath. Remove finished spikes to keep the clump flowering. Rich flame-orange and red pokers make a fiery show in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Give this giant room; plant in spring after frost.

directions: Plant the large crown at soil level in full sun and stake the towering stems. Cut old flower stalks to the base and clear spent leaves in spring. Enormous orange-red pokers dominate the late-summer border.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a tall, showy bicolor for the border.

directions: Plant the crown level with the soil in full sun and stake in exposed spots. Deadhead spent spikes and tidy old foliage before spring growth. Scarlet buds open to bright yellow on tall, sturdy stems in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; an early, robust old variety.

directions: Plant the vigorous crown at soil level in full sun with room to spread. Remove faded pokers and clear old leaves in early spring. Red buds opening to yellow appear early, often in late spring into summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a tall, deep-red heirloom.

directions: Plant the crown level with the soil in full sun and stake tall stems if needed. Deadhead spent pokers and remove old foliage in spring. Deep scarlet-red spikes rise on strong stems in midsummer.

common_name: Pyromania Orange Blaze Red Hot Poker

planting.note: Plant in spring; a strongly reblooming selection.

directions: Plant the crown at soil level in full sun with sharp drainage. Cut spent spikes to trigger repeat flushes through summer. Bright orange pokers rebloom reliably over a long season.

planting.note: Plant in spring for a fiery, repeat-blooming clump.

directions: Plant the crown level with the soil in full sun and keep drainage sharp. Deadhead to encourage a long run of repeat bloom. Deep orange-red buds open to golden yellow, glowing like embers through summer.

common_name: Pyromania Rocket's Red Glare Red Hot Poker

planting.note: Plant in spring; a vivid red reblooming variety.

directions: Plant the crown at soil level in full sun with good drainage. Remove spent spikes promptly to keep it reblooming. Bold red-orange pokers make a strong show over a long summer season.

common_name: Pyromania Hot and Cold Red Hot Poker

planting.note: Plant in spring; a striking bicolor rebloomer.

directions: Plant the crown level with the soil in full sun with sharp drainage. Deadhead through the season to prompt repeat bloom. Dark stems carry pokers of warm coral above and cool white below, opening through summer.

planting.note: Plant after frost; tolerates seasonally moist ground.

directions: Sow seed under a thin covering and grow seedlings on before planting out. This grassland species takes summer moisture and makes a strong upright clump. Dense orange-and-yellow pokers rise in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a robust, evergreen-leaved species.

directions: Barely cover seed and keep warm, then grow on before planting the broad-leaved clumps. This sturdy species holds its foliage well and asks for sun and drainage. Pale greenish-yellow to cream pokers open over summer.

planting.note: Plant after frost; the tallest species needs space and shelter.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and grow seedlings on for a season before planting the imposing clumps. This giant species produces exceptionally long, densely packed spikes and wants a sheltered, sunny site. Slender white-to-yellow pokers tower in late summer and autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring into a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Cover seed lightly and grow on before setting the clumps into full sun. This mountain-grassland species is reliably hardy and needs little care beyond good drainage. Bicolor coral-and-yellow pokers open in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a very compact, reblooming dwarf.

directions: Plant the crown at soil level in full sun with sharp drainage. Deadhead the small spikes to keep this dwarf reblooming through the season. Compact orange pokers appear over a long summer window.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a dwarf yellow for the front edge.

directions: Set the crown level with the soil in full sun and keep it well drained. Remove finished spikes to encourage repeat bloom. Neat lemon-yellow pokers rise on this compact, tidy dwarf all summer.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter and set plants out around the last frost.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep it lightly moist until sprouts appear in two to four weeks. Give plants a lean, well-drained soil and stake the wiry stems in richer ground. Crimson pincushion flowers open from early summer and keep coming if you deadhead spent blooms.
